title: Sleep well: the medicine we always forget
summary: Sleep is not a luxury. It's one of the most powerful medicines we have — and it's free. Yet we sacrifice it every day.
content:
## The patient who doesn't sleep

They walk into the office, sit down, and before they even tell me what brought them here, they yawn. Well — they yawn, but I read it as a reminder to myself.

How often, during a consultation, does it emerge that the patient sleeps five hours a night but "has got used to it"? Too often.

Sleep deprivation is one of the most underestimated conditions in primary care. It doesn't hurt acutely, doesn't send anyone to A&E, and doesn't show up in blood tests. Yet it quietly and systematically erodes health.

## What happens when we don't sleep

During sleep, the brain doesn't switch off: **it works**. It consolidates memory, clears metabolic waste products accumulated during the day (including beta-amyloid protein, linked to Alzheimer's), regulates hormones and repairs tissues.

Chronic sleep of less than 7 hours per night is associated with:

- **Increased cardiovascular risk** — hypertension, heart attack, stroke
- **Blood sugar disruption** — reduced insulin sensitivity, risk of type 2 diabetes
- **Weakened immune system** — getting ill more easily
- **Weight gain** — sleep regulates ghrelin and leptin, the hunger hormones
- **Mood and anxiety** — chronic fatigue amplifies negative emotions

## How many hours is "enough"?

For most adults: **7–9 hours**. This isn't an average: it's a physiological range. Below 6 hours, the damage is documented even in people who claim to "function just fine" — perceived adaptation does not equal genuine recovery.

## Five concrete things you can do

**1. Fixed schedule, even on weekends.**
The circadian rhythm is a biological clock: shift it every Saturday and you confuse it. Getting up at the same time every day matters more than when you go to bed.

**2. No screens in the hour before bed.**
Blue light from phones blocks melatonin. Replace your evening scroll with something analogue: a book, music, a short walk.

**3. Your bedroom should be dark, cool and quiet.**
The ideal sleeping temperature is between 16 and 19°C. Even a small light source (a TV standby light, a gap under the door) can interfere with sleep quality.

**4. Caffeine: watch the timing.**
The half-life of caffeine is around 5–6 hours. A coffee at 4 pm means half the caffeine is still circulating at midnight. Everyone has their own sensitivity — but it's worth experimenting.

**5. If you can't sleep, get up.**
Lying awake in bed anxiously trying to sleep makes things worse. Get up, do something relaxing in dim light, and only return to bed when you feel sleepy. The bed should be associated with sleep, not frustrated wakefulness.

## A final note

If you have persistent difficulty falling or staying asleep, talk to your doctor. Chronic insomnia is treatable — and Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) is now considered the first-line treatment, more effective than medication in the long term.

Sleep is non-negotiable. Stop treating it as optional.

title: Welcome to my new website
summary: A new space where I share thoughts on health, nutrition and everyday well-being.
content:
## A new beginning

Welcome to this corner of the web that I've made entirely my own.

After years in clinical practice, I felt the need for a freer space to write about health differently: without the rush of a consultation, without time constraints — with the ability to explore, share stories and reason together.

## What you'll find here

In this blog I'll write about:

- **Nutrition** — why we eat what we eat, and how to do it better
- **Primary care** — the questions patients ask me most often
- **Prevention** — small daily habits with a big impact on health
- **Well-being** — the blurred boundary between body and mind

## A promise

I'll always try to write clearly, without unnecessary technical jargon, with reliable sources and a little extra honesty.

See you soon.

title: Iron in your diet: everything you need to know
summary: Iron deficiency, food sources and practical tips to better absorb this essential mineral.
content:
## Why is iron so important?

Iron is an essential mineral for our body. Its main function is to carry oxygen in the blood through haemoglobin, the protein found in red blood cells.

Iron deficiency leads to **iron-deficiency anaemia**, one of the most widespread forms of anaemia in the world. Symptoms include:

- fatigue and weakness
- paleness
- difficulty concentrating
- brittle hair and nails

## Two types of dietary iron

### Haem iron
Found in animal-based foods: red meat, liver, fish, shellfish. It is absorbed very efficiently by the gut (15–35% of ingested iron).

### Non-haem iron
Found in plant foods: legumes (lentils, beans, chickpeas), spinach, tofu, nuts and seeds. Absorption is lower (2–20%), but can be improved with a few simple tricks.

## How to boost iron absorption

**Pair it with vitamin C.** Lemon juice, raw bell pepper, tomatoes — add them to iron-rich vegetable dishes to nearly triple absorption.

**Avoid certain combinations.** Tea, coffee and milk reduce iron absorption when consumed with meals.

**Brief cooking.** Vegetables cooked too long lose some of their nutrients, iron included.

## When is a supplement needed?

Iron supplements should only be prescribed after a proper diagnosis with blood tests. Taking iron without medical need is not without risks. If in doubt, talk to your doctor.
